plainMeaning: In each of the five great Mahāvideha lands four awakened teachers are alive right now, and this verse names the first four of them — Sīmandhar, Jugmandhar, Bāhu and Subāhu — as givers of joy.
recitation: (ḍhāḷa-cothī) mahāvideha pañca majhāra, pratyeke jina cāra. sīmandhara jugamandhara, bāhu subāhu e sukhakara.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ḍhāḷa 'tune, melodic stanza-set' (cf. Sanskrit dhārā/Prakrit ḍhāla) + cothī 'fourth' (Skt. caturthī).
wordByWord.notes: A performance rubric, not a word of the prayer; it marks the opening of the fourth dhāla.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit mahā- 'great' + videha, the central continent-region; a glossary term carried from the preface.
wordByWord.notes: Used here collectively for the five Mahāvideha sections.

wordByWord.etymology: Apabhraṁśa/old Gujarati majhāra, from Sanskrit madhya 'middle' (via Prakrit majjha); a postposition of location.
wordByWord.notes: Governs the preceding 'five Mahāvideha': 'within the five Mahāvidehas'.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit prati- 'each, towards' + eka 'one', locative pratyeke 'in each individually'.
wordByWord.notes: Distributes 'four Jinas' over each of the five regions.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit jina 'conqueror', from √ji 'to conquer'; a glossary term from the preface.
wordByWord.notes: Here the living (viharamāṇa) Jinas; the singular form stands for the class.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati cāra from Sanskrit catur 'four' (via Prakrit caü).
wordByWord.notes: Five regions × four Jinas yields the twenty living Jinas.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sīma(n) 'boundary, summit' + √dhṛ 'to hold, bear' — 'upholder of the boundary/order'.
wordByWord.notes: The most widely venerated of the twenty viharamāṇa Jinas, residing in Mahāvidehakṣetra.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit yugma/yuga 'pair, age' + √dhṛ 'to bear' — 'bearer of the age/pair'; Prakritized juga- for yuga-.
wordByWord.notes: Standardized form Yugmandhara; the book's juga- spelling is kept verbatim.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bāhu 'arm', here a proper name.
Sanskrit su- 'good, fair' + bāhu 'arm' — 'fair-armed'; a proper name.
wordByWord.notes: Bāhu and Subāhu are named as a natural pair, as their forms suggest.

wordByWord.etymology: Old/colloquial Gujarati demonstrative e 'this, these' (cf. Sanskrit eṣa/ete).
wordByWord.notes: Points back to the four just-named Jinas.

wordByWord.gloss: givers of joy, makers of well-being
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sukha 'happiness, ease' + -kara 'making, causing' (√kṛ 'to do, make') — 'producing happiness'.
wordByWord.notes: Predicate epithet of the four: contemplating them brings the devotee sukha.

literalTranslation: (Fourth dhāla.) Within the five Mahāvidehas, four Jinas in each; Sīmandhar, Jugmandhar, Bāhu, Subāhu — these are givers of joy.
idiomaticTranslation: Across the five great Mahāvideha lands, in every one of them four awakened Jinas are alive and teaching. Sīmandhar and Jugmandhar, Bāhu and Subāhu — these, the first of them, are bestowers of bliss; to recall them is itself a joy.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Opening the fourth dhāla, the hymn turns from the merely-named future Jinas of the previous verse to Jinas who are alive at this very moment. The arithmetic is deliberate and devotional: pañca majhāra ('within the five') times pratyeke jina cāra ('four in each') gives the twenty viharamāṇa Jinas of the Mahāvideha realms. Where the past, present and future caubīsīs honoured Tīrthaṅkaras who have departed, are gone, or are yet to come, this set is being adored as present and accessible — which is why the poet immediately starts to list them by name. He gives the first four here: Sīmandhar, Jugmandhar, Bāhu, Subāhu, the head of the roll and (in Sīmandhar) the single most beloved living Jina of Śvetāmbara devotion. The closing epithet e sukhakar ('these are givers of joy') is the verse's emotional hinge: their names are not data to be counted but a source of sukha to the one who utters them. The verse is grammatically a single breath — region, count, names, blessing — and the following verse simply continues the same list (Sujāt, Svayamprabha, Ṛṣabhānana, Anantavīrya, Surprabhu), so this stanza should be chanted as the first half of an unbroken naming of the living Jinas.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the doctrine of the viharamāṇa (currently-living) Jinas: while Bharata-kṣetra is in a descending age in which no Tīrthaṅkara is presently born, the five Mahāvideha regions abide perpetually in a fourth-ara-like condition where awakened Jinas are always present and preaching. The cosmos therefore is never without a living ford-maker, and liberation-teaching is never wholly inaccessible. Naming four of the twenty here, with Sīmandhar foremost, expresses the lived bhakti corollary of that doctrine — the practitioner can bow to a Jina who exists now, not only to images of the departed — which directly strengthens samyaktva (right faith) by anchoring it in a presently-real refuge.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When reciting, let the names slow you down rather than rush the count: 'four in each of five' is an invitation to feel that an awakened guide is alive somewhere right now. Many reciters pause on Sīmandhar with a personal salutation. Take the closing word sukhakar as instruction — receive the joy the verse offers instead of treating the figures as a tally to verify.

crossReferences.text: Śrīmandara-stavana (hymn to Sīmandharasvāmī, on the twenty viharamāṇa Tīrthaṅkaras)
crossReferences.source: Ṛṣi Rāmakṛṣṇa; Tessitori Collection, Biblioteca Civica 'V. Joppi', Udine

crossReferences.text: Jaina cosmography of Mahāvideha (five regions, four living Jinas each = twenty)

sources.title: Simandhar (Simandhara) — Tīrthaṅkara currently living in Mahāvideh Kṣetra
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the five Mahāvideh kṣetras with four Tīrthaṅkaras each (twenty total), and Sīmandhar's residence and identity.

sources.title: Viharamāna — definitions (the twenty living Tīrthaṃkaras)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying viharamāṇa = the twenty living Jinas and Sīmandhara as foremost among them in Mahāvidehakṣetra.

sources.title: Sukhakara — Sanskrit dictionary definitions
sources.consultedFor: Gloss and etymology of sukha-kara 'giving pleasure, producing happiness' for the epithet sukhakar.

notes: The heading (ઢાળ-ચોથી) is a performance rubric marking the fourth dhāla, not part of the prayer's wording; it is glossed but set apart. જુગમંધર is the Prakritized/book spelling of standard Yugmandhara (Jugmandhar). મઝાર is an Apabhraṁśa/old-Gujarati locative postposition (< Skt. madhya). The verse names only the first four of the twenty viharamāṇa Jinas; the count is completed across this and the following verse. જિન is singular in form but collective in sense ('Jinas'). Spellings are preserved verbatim per the book's orthography; the daṇḍa '।' and the period before line breaks are book punctuation. Bāhu/Subāhu are read as a name-pair on the basis of their forms; no variant readings were flagged for this stanza in the consulted sources.
